title-group.article-title: Neurological manifestations with jugular vein thrombosis linked to an inflammatory profile may be a sequela of long COVID
title-group.alt-title: JUGULAR VEIN THROMBOSIS AS A PROBABLE SEQUELA OF LONG COVID

aff.institution: Instituto Nacional de Neurología y Neurocirugía Manuel Velasco Suarez

aff.named-content: Institute for Memory Impairments and Neurological Disorders

aff.institution: Secretaría de Salud de la Ciudad de México
Facultad de Estudios Superiores Iztacala, UNAM

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: More than 670 million cases of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID‐19) have been recorded worldwide in the 3 years since the start of the severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS‐CoV‐2) pandemic. About 45% of survivors of COVID‐19 develop a syndrome known as long‐term COVID, in which symptoms persist even months after the acute infection. About 76% of patients with long COVID experience neurological manifestations. Moreover, patients who have survived COVID‐19 have an increased risk of cerebral venous thrombosis. This case report describes a 41‐year‐old woman who developed neurological manifestations associated with jugular vein thrombosis 24 h after administration of the Oxford–AstraZeneca (ChAdOx1 nCoV‐19) vaccine (AstraZeneca‐Serum Institute of India). She had been infected with SARS‐CoV‐2 three months before vaccination. Although initially suspected to be a case of vaccine‐induced immune thrombotic thrombocytopenia (VITT) in view of her recent vaccination, the patient did not have any hallmarks of VITT, such as thrombocytopenia, an increased d‐dimer level, or antibodies against platelet factor‐4. Moreover, the neurological manifestations were associated with a high concentration of inflammatory cytokines, including interleukin (IL)‐6, IL‐17A, and IL‐21, and elevated neutrophil levels in cerebrospinal fluid, suggesting that inflammatory immune components had a role in the development of thrombotic events and pointing to an alternative diagnosis. In this case, the laboratory results indicated that the neurological manifestations associated with jugular vein thrombosis were not associated with VITT. Therefore, we propose that the thrombosis of the left jugular vein was a sequela of SARS‐CoV‐2 infection.

Weeks or months after acute infection with severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2, the patient presented increased levels of pro‐inflammatory cytokines and neutrophils in the cerebrospinal fluid and blood, with jugular vein thrombosis as a possible sequela of long coronavirus disease 2019.

list-item.p: We describe a case of neurological manifestations with jugular vein thrombosis (NMjT) developed after 3 months of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS‐CoV‐2) infection.
NMjT linked to inflammation might be a manifestation of a long COVID sequela.
We propose that idiopathic NMjT might be considered as long COVID sequelae if the patient coursed with inflammation and had been previously diagnosed with SARS‐CoV‐2 infection.

institution-wrap.institution: Initiative Innova‐UNAM and Alianza UCMX of the University of California

p.mixed-citation: Mendoza‐Portillo E , Aleman‐Navarro E , Prieto GA , Rosenstein Y , Lozano‐Nuevo JJ , Perez‐Lopez A . Neurological manifestations with jugular vein thrombosis linked to an inflammatory profile may be a sequela of long COVID. Neuroprotection. 2023;1:170‐175. 10.1002/nep3.24

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: More than 670 million cases of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID‐19) were reported by January 2023, 1 and about 45% of survivors report unremitting symptoms months after diagnosis. 2 The wide range of symptoms that persist weeks after acute severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS‐CoV‐2) infection is known as long COVID. 2 About 76% of patients with long COVID experience neurological manifestations. 3 These sequelae include cerebrovascular and cognitive disorders, as well as an increased risk of cerebral venous thrombosis (CVT) (hazard ratio 2.69, 95% confidence interval 1.29–5.62). 4 CVT has been reported to be a complication after COVID‐19 vaccination, 5 especially with the adenovirus vector vaccine ChAdOx1 (Oxford–AstraZeneca). 5 In this report, we describe a case of neurological manifestations associated with jugular vein thrombosis (NMjT) that occurred 24 h after administration of the ChAdOx1 nCoV‐19 vaccine (AstraZeneca‐Serum Institute of India).

sec.title: CASE REPORT
sec.p: A 41‐year‐old woman with a medical history that was unremarkable except for infection with SARS‐CoV‐2 3 months before vaccination was admitted to the emergency room within 24 h of receiving the first dose of the ChAdOx1 nCoV‐19 vaccine. She presented with nausea, vomiting, and an 8/10 holocranial headache, which worsened with the Valsalva maneuver. The initial physical examination revealed a slightly diminished level of consciousness characterized by drowsiness, preserved campimetry, a centered primary gaze, and symmetrical 3‐mm pupils with a direct and consensual response. The patient had a positive corneal reflex, facial symmetry at rest and after the Foix maneuver, and a bilateral gag reflex. Muscle strength was decreased on the right, and there was an extensor plantar response on both sides with increased muscle tone secondary to right‐sided spasticity.

Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and blood samples were collected within 4 h of admission. Laboratory investigations revealed a normal platelet count, fibrinogen level, prothrombin time, international normalized ratio, partial thromboplastin time, and d‐dimer level. A test for platelet factor‐4/heparin complex (PF4/heparin) antibodies was negative (optical density, 0.034) (Table 1). No infectious agents were detected. CSF was negative for anti‐cysticercus antibodies (enzyme‐linked immunosorbent assay) and nonreactive for Escherichia coli K1, Haemophilus influenzae, Listeria monocytogenes, Neisseria meningitidis, Streptococcus agalactiae, cytomegalovirus, enterovirus, herpes simplex virus 1, herpes simplex virus 2, human herpesvirus 6, human parechovirus, varicella zoster virus, and Cryptococcus neoformans/gattii (multiplex polymerase chain reaction, FilmArray). A polymerase chain reaction test for SARS‐CoV‐2 was negative.

caption.p: Laboratory values of blood and CSF samples collected within 4 h of admission.

sec.p: Complementary studies showed a CSF opening pressure of 210 mm H2O. Analysis of CSF revealed a low glucose level, an increased total protein concentration, and hemorrhage. Consistent with an abnormal surface electroencephalogram and sleepiness possibly associated with moderate to severe generalized dysfunction (Figure 1), magnetic resonance venography of the brain and neck showed a filling defect in the left jugular bulb with CVT in the left jugular vein and occlusion of the main venous drainage system of the brain (Figure 2). Although CVT and jugular vein thrombosis are anatomically different entities, both cause increased intracranial pressure and explain the patient's clinical symptoms, signs, and changes in the CSF.

caption.p: Surface electroencephalogram with a 10–20 electrodes system showing recordings of the patient in drowsiness, with a baseline activity in the theta range of 5 to 6 Hz and an amplitude of 20–30 μV, synchronous, symmetrical, without anteroposterior gradient integration. Frequent outbreaks of slow waves, polymorphous, in generalized delta frequency of 1.5–3 Hz (red square) were observed suggestive of encephalopathy with abnormal neuron responses.
Magnetic resonance imaging in the venous phase showing the contrast medium (gadolinium) filling defect in the bulbar region of the left jugular portion (arrow).

sec.p: The total leukocyte count in CSF was 26 cells/mL. Notably, flow cytometry revealed that 91.6% of cells in the CSF were neutrophils (CD45+, CD11b+, and CD16+) and 7% were monocytes/macrophages (CD45+, CD11b+, and CD16−) (Figure 3A), with normal neutrophil and monocyte/macrophage ratios in peripheral blood (63.8% and 6.1%, respectively) (Figure 3B). Quantification of a panel of 12 cytokines revealed elevated levels of interleukin (IL)‐6 (5766 ± 547 pg/mL), IL1‐7A (58 ± 14 pg/mL), and IL‐10 (36 ± 2 pg/mL) in CSF relative to control values reported for noninflammatory neurological diseases 7 and values found in the CSF of a patient with Guillain‐Barré syndrome who was used as a control for neuroinflammation. The patient's serum contained 5–8‐fold increases in concentrations of all cytokines in the panel (IL‐5, IL‐13, lL‐2, IL‐6, IL‐9, IL‐10, interferon gamma, tumor necrosis factor‐alpha, IL‐17A, IL‐17F, IL‐4, IL‐21, and IL‐22) relative to two normal age‐ and sex‐matched serum samples (Figure 4). Overall, the CSF/serum ratio indicated that the patient's CSF contained > 400 times more IL‐17A, >80 times more IL‐6, and >2.8 times more IL‐10 when compared with normal values. The high cytokine levels in blood, infiltration of neutrophils, and elevated IL‐6, IL‐17A, and IL‐10 levels in CSF suggested a systemic immune response impacting the central nervous sytem.

caption.p: Cell analysis in CSF and peripheral blood. After gating for live CD45+ events, cells obtained from CSF and whole blood were analyzed by flow cytometry to evaluate the presence of (A) neutrophils (CD11b+, CD16+ cells), and (B) B (CD19+ cells) and T cells (CD3+ cells). Dot plots are presented. CSF, cerebrospinal fluid.

Cytokine analysis in CSF and serum. (A) Cytokine in the CSF of the patient (CVT), as well as in the CSF of a Guillain‐Barré syndrome (GBS) patient (included for comparison with an immune‐related CNS disease). (B) Serum cytokines concentrations in CVT and GBS patients and two healthy donors (38 and 29‐years‐old women, control‐1 and ‐2, respectively). Cytokine quantification was performed with a LEGENDplex immunoassay (BioLegend). All samples were analyzed in triplicate; bars represent the mean ± SE. #nondetected. CSF, cerebrospinal fluid; CVT, cerebral venous thrombosis; SE, standard error of mean.

sec.p: The patient received unfractionated heparin at a dose of 1 mg/kg. However, on the second hospital day, she developed cerebral edema, which was treated with hyperosmolar therapy (sodium chloride 7%, administered intravenously). The patient's cerebral edema resolved within 38 days, by which time she had made satisfactory clinical progress. Assessment of mental orientation showed that the patient was awake, alert, and oriented with preserved semantic memory, judgment, abstraction, and calculation but also detected impaired episodic memory performance. The communication assessment showed no motor or sensory aphasia. Imaging studies did not reveal alterations in the cranial nerves. The scores for proximal and distal muscle strength were 5/5, and muscle tone and trophism were preserved together with gait. The patient did not have any abnormal movements or meningeal signs. A 10‐week follow‐up confirmed total recovery, including episodic memory capacity.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: Cerebral thrombotic events have been reported after SARS‐CoV‐2 vaccination and mainly with the adenovirus‐based ChAdOx1 nCoV‐19 (Oxford–AstraZeneca) and Ad26.COV2.S (Johnson & Johnson/Janssen) vaccines. 6 , 7 Considering that antibodies against SARS‐CoV‐2 surface proteins (e.g., spike) induced by SARS‐CoV‐2 vaccination may cross‐react with epitopes expressed on the platelet surface (molecular mimicry), 8 potentially favoring clotting, we first considered the possibility that the patient's neurological manifestations were associated with VITT induced by the Oxford–AstraZeneca vaccine. The diagnostic criteria for CVT associated with VITT include CVT verified by neuroimaging within 5–30 days after SARS‐CoV‐2 vaccination, thrombocytopenia (lowest platelet count <150 × 109/L or 50% reduction from baseline), and presence of anti‐PF4 antibodies. 9 Although our patient was confirmed to have jugular thrombosis by magnetic resonance imaging within 24 h following SARS‐CoV‐2 vaccination, none of the additional criteria for VITT were met. Her platelet count, fibrinogen level, and prothrombin time were within the normal ranges, and her anti‐PF4 antibody test was negative. Hence, even though a multicenter cohort study in 2021 found that 27% of cases of CVT associated with SARS‐CoV‐2 vaccination did not meet all the criteria for VITT (as in our patient) and were referred to as non‐VITT, 10 we propose the following alternative hypothesis as an explanation for this atypical presentation of NMjT.

CVT is a rare vascular disease that mainly affects young women. In addition to the common risk factors (pregnancy, puerperium, hormonal contraceptive therapy, neoplasms, infections, obesity, and thrombophilia), CVT has been increasingly associated with COVID‐19. Even 12 months after the initial COVID‐19 infection, patients are at increased risk of cardiovascular events (hazard ratio 1.45–1.61), including cerebrovascular events. 11 In contrast, the occurrence of CVT associated with VITT is estimated to be about 1:50,000 in people under 50 years of age who are immunized with the Oxford–AstraZeneca vaccine, 9 and the occurrence of non‐VITT CVT is expected to be even lower, which supports the possibility that the NMjT in our patient was a sequela of long COVID.

According to emerging data, inflammation is a major mechanism underlying long COVID. 12 Notably, our patient had elevated levels of several pro‐inflammatory cytokines in both CSF and serum, particularly IL‐6, which is produced by endothelial cells and numerous immune cells that stimulates production of acute‐phase mediators and chemoattractants during venous thrombotic events, 13 , 14 , 15 thereby amplifying the inflammatory process. Consistent with these high levels of pro‐inflammatory cytokines, the patient's CSF contained a significant leukocyte infiltrate consisting of neutrophils ( > 90%) and monocytes (7%). Although neutrophils are prevented from trafficking into the CSF under homeostatic conditions, they can infiltrate the central nervous system under certain pathological conditions. 16 Neutrophils, monocytes (a potential source of tissue factor), and platelets play a central role in the physiopathology of thrombotic events, with activated platelets trapped in neutrophil extracellular traps promoting formation of thrombus. 17 , 18 , 19 Overall, the cytokine and immune cell patterns in our patient's CSF and serum are consistent with an NMjT associated with inflammation.

Although early detection of immune markers in CSF and serum (within 4 h of admission) may confirm a pre‐inflammatory state, the possibility that jugular vein thrombosis triggered the inflammatory response in our patient cannot be excluded. Neurological manifestations are just one of the many abnormalities observed in patients with long COVID, the characteristics, spectrum, and significance of which are not yet fully understood. Recent reports indicate that about 33% of patients infected with SARS‐CoV‐2 are diagnosed with a neurological or psychiatric illness within 6 months following acute COVID‐19. 20 The patient described here contracted COVID‐19 3 months before receiving the Oxford–AstraZeneca vaccine, and 24 h after vaccination developed thrombosis in the left jugular vein. According to laboratory tests, her NMjT was not associated with VITT; however, it could have been a manifestation of inflammation related to long COVID. Considering the high number of COVID cases recorded worldwide, the contribution of long COVID should be considered in cases of idiopathic NMjT after COVID vaccination.

sec.title: AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
sec.p: Elizabeth Mendoza‐Portillo: Conceptualization (equal); data curation (equal); formal analysis (equal); methodology (equal). Estefania Aleman‐Navarro: Data curation (equal); methodology (equal). G. Aleph Prieto: Conceptualization (equal); data curation (equal); formal analysis (equal); funding acquisition (equal); investigation (equal); methodology (equal); project administration (equal); resources (equal); supervision (equal); visualization (equal); writing—original draft (equal); writing—review & editing (equal). Yvonne Rosenstein: Conceptualization (equal); data curation (equal); formal analysis (equal); funding acquisition (equal); investigation (equal); methodology (equal); project administration (equal); resources (equal); supervision (equal); writing—original draft (equal); writing—review & editing (equal). Jose Juan Lozano‐Nuevo: Conceptualization (equal); data curation (equal); formal analysis (equal); investigation (equal); methodology (equal); writing—review & editing (equal). sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest.

sec.title: ETHICS STATEMENT
sec.p: Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication of this case report.

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
ack.p: We thank the technical assistance of Erika Melchy (Flow Cytometry Unit, IBT/UNAM) and of the Flow Cytometry Unit at the Red de Apoyo a la Investigación‐UNAM/Instituto Nacional de Ciencias Médicas y Nutrición Salvador Zubirán. This work was supported by UNAM‐DGAPA‐PAPIIT (grant IA208222) and the initiative Innova‐UNAM and Alianza UCMX of the University of California to APL, the Alzheimer's Association (grant AARF‐21‐72286) and UNAM‐DGAPA‐PAPIIT (grant IA203421) to G.A.P, and UNAM‐DGAPA‐PAPIIT (grant IN222523) and CONACYT (grant A1‐S‐15601) to YR.

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
sec.p: All the data generated in this study is included in the main manuscript. All data produced in the present work are contained in the manuscript and are available upon reasonable request.
